rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON J. WILLIAMS REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 10/5/99 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Transfer Action To General Civil Division (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 2] Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on March 30, 2021. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 2 of 2]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON J. WILLIAMS REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 10/5/99 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer Action To General Civil Division
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Respondent Betty Williams's ("Betty") Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED. (See Evid. Code, sections 452, 453.) Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) Betty's Motion to Transfer Action to General Civil Division is DENIED. Shrita Williams, Gloria Williams and Lillian Williams Jackson ("Beneficiaries") are beneficiaries of the Leon J. Williams Revocable Trust, dated October 5, 1999 ("Trust") and heirs of decedent Leon J. Williams ("Leon"). Beneficiaries filed the Second Amended Petition for Transfer and Conveyance of Trust Real Property; for Declaratory Relief; Imposition of Constructive Trust; and for Adverse Possession ("Petition") seeking to establish that based on an agreement between Betty and Leon or adverse possession the 100 percent real property at 252 Minerva Street is a Trust asset or, alternatively, an asset of Leon's estate. As such, the Petition addresses the internal affairs of the Trust. (Probate Code, section 17200, subd. (b)(2); see also Prob. Code, sections 800; 801; 850, subd. (a)(3)(B); 17000; and 17200, Estate of Heggstad (1993) 16 Cal. App. 4th 943, 951-952.).) "The superior court having jurisdiction over the trust pursuant to this part has exclusive jurisdiction of proceedings concerning the internal affairs of trusts." (Prob. C. section 17000(a).) To the extent Betty is asking the court to sever the adverse possession claim and refer it to the housing court, the court declines to so exercise its discretionary authority under Section 801. The petition also addresses the administration of Leon's estate, a subject over which this court has jurisdiction. (See Prob. Code, section 7050; see Law Revision Comm'n Comment to Section 7050 ["Where appropriate, the reference to the superior court in [Section 7050]  means the DEPARTMENT OR JUDGE OF THE COURT that deals with probate matters" (emphasis added).) "The court, on its own motion or on the motion of any interested party, may order that an action or proceeding not specifically provided in this code be determined in a separate civil action." (Prob. Code, section 801.) The claims in the Petition are closely related and commonly heard in this department. On the record before it, the court cannot say that transfer of any part of the Petition to the housing court will promote efficient and orderly adjudication of the pending claims. In re Marriage of Perry (1997) 58 Cal. App. 4th 1104 is inapposite. There, the appellate court "[was] satisfied" that the family court was "a correct forum" for the creditor's claim for child support payments from trust asset because the probate court had only concurrent, not exclusive, jurisdiction over "proceedings" by "creditors . . . of trusts." under section 17000(b)(2) and focus was a dispute regarding child support. (Perry, supra, 58 Cal.App.4th at p. 1111.) Here, the probate court has exclusive jurisdiction over Beneficiaries' claims addressing the internal affairs of the trust. Moreover, as in Perry, another department with concurrent jurisdiction may be "a correct forum" for a claim in the Petition, but that doesn't mean that the Petition or an individual claim must be transferred and, as stated, the court declines to exercise its discretion in favor of transfer. Beneficiaries shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a hard-copy proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling. [Part 1 of 2]
